metric: Effective firm capacity already built globally (GW) = global installed nameplate capacity (end-2024) x technology capacity credit (firm-capacity contribution / ELCC capacity value)
normalization: Log10 of effective firm capacity in GW, with fixed end-points: 1 GW maps to 0 and 1000 GW maps to 1. score = clip( log10(firm_GW) / 3 , 0, 1 ). (So 10 GW -> 0.33, 100 GW -> 0.67, 1000 GW -> 1.0.) Capacity credits used: reservoir hydro 50% (moderate-high, reservoir-weighted), nuclear 95%, geothermal 90%, gas CCGT 80% (all per PJM ELCC class ratings / baseload capacity factors), solar+wind blended ~13% (ELCC ~8-20%); 4h battery firms only its short duration so the portfolio stays low; long-duration storage portfolios are essentially unbuilt globally (sub-GW of non-pumped LDES) so their delivered firm capacity is near zero.

scores.metric_value: ~642 GW firm (1283 GW nameplate renewable hydro, excl. pumped storage, x ~50% reservoir capacity credit)
scores.rationale: Largest single firm-capacity fleet after gas: huge nameplate (1283 GW) with moderate-high reservoir capacity value, derated for run-of-river/seasonal limits.

sources.name: IRENA Renewable Capacity Highlights 2025 (end-2024: 1283 GW renewable hydro, excl. 142 GW pumped storage)
PJM ELCC Class Ratings 2026/27 BRA (Hydro Intermittent 38-40%; reservoir hydro higher)

scores.metric_value: ~358 GW firm (377 GW nameplate, 417 reactors, x 95% capacity credit)
scores.rationale: Large dispatchable baseload fleet with near-full capacity credit (PJM nuclear 95%); SMR contribution still negligible in 2024 nameplate.

sources.name: IAEA / WNA: 417 reactors, ~377 GW operational end-2024
PJM ELCC Class Ratings (Nuclear 95%)

scores.metric_value: ~12.9 GW firm (14.3 GW nameplate x ~90% baseload capacity credit)
scores.rationale: High capacity credit (baseload, ~90% capacity factor) but tiny global nameplate (14 GW), so absolute firm capacity is small; EGS still pre-commercial.

sources.name: IRENA Renewable Capacity Highlights 2025 (geothermal 14.3 GW end-2024)
NREL / Stanford: geothermal baseload capacity factor >90%

scores.metric_value: ~390 GW firm (1865 GW solar + 1133 GW wind = ~2998 GW nameplate x ~13% blended capacity credit)
scores.rationale: Massive nameplate (~3000 GW) but a 4h battery only firms the evening peak; blended ELCC ~8-20% (solar 8-11%, wind 13-41%) collapses effective firm capacity to ~13% of nameplate.

sources.name: IRENA Renewable Capacity Highlights 2025 (solar 1865 GW, wind 1133 GW end-2024)
PJM ELCC Class Ratings (Fixed solar 8%, Tracking solar 11%, Onshore wind 41%, 4-hr Storage 50%)
Capacity credit (Wikipedia): GB solar ~1.2%, onshore wind ~8%; values fall with penetration

scores.technology: Solar plus wind with long-duration storage
scores.metric_value: ~2 GW firm (solar+wind paired with deployed non-pumped LDES, which is sub-GW globally)
scores.rationale: As a firmed system it is essentially unbuilt: utility battery fleet is ~124 GW but mostly 4h, and true long-duration (8h+, non-PSH) storage is sub-GW, so almost no firm VRE has actually been delivered this way.

sources.name: IEA Global Energy Review (utility battery ~124 GW end-2024, ~90% LFP, mostly short-duration)
PJM ELCC Class Ratings (10-hr Storage 72%, 4-hr 50% - longer duration earns more credit but is barely deployed)
UCS: ELCC of storage rises with duration but declines with penetration

scores.metric_value: ~800 GW firm (~1000 GW CCGT nameplate, ~55% of ~1800 GW global gas-fired, x ~80% capacity credit)
scores.rationale: Largest delivered firm-capacity fleet: very large dispatchable nameplate with high (~80%) capacity credit per PJM Gas Combined Cycle ELCC.

sources.name: Energy Institute / IEA: ~1800 GW global gas-fired capacity end-2024 (CCGT ~55-60%)
PJM ELCC Class Ratings (Gas Combined Cycle 74-80%)

metric: Flexibility composite (dimensionless, 0-1) built from three sub-metrics: (a) max sustained ramp rate in % of rated capacity per minute; (b) provides true synchronous (rotating-mass) inertia [1/0]; (c) black-start capable [1/0].
normalization: Composite = 0.5*norm(ramp) + 0.25*inertia + 0.25*blackstart. norm(ramp) = min(ramp_%_per_min / 60, 1), i.e. a ramp of >=60 %/min maps to 1.0 and 0 %/min to 0.0 (linear in between). inertia and blackstart are 1 if the technology natively provides true synchronous inertia / black-start, else 0. Composite therefore ranges 0 (no ramp, no inertia, no black-start) to 1.0 (>=60 %/min ramp + synchronous inertia + black-start). Ramp values used: hydro 30, batteries effectively instantaneous (>=60 -> 1.0), geothermal 15, CCGT 10, nuclear 5.

scores.metric_value: ~30 %/min ramp (range 10-30+ %/min; near-instant in seconds); synchronous inertia = 1; black-start = 1
scores.rationale: Fast ramp (norm 0.50) plus the two booleans: hydro provides true rotating-mass inertia and is the premier black-start resource (DOE designates it a primary black-start technology).

sources.name: NREL Greening the Grid - hydro 10-30 %/min flexibility
US DOE/ORNL - Hydropower Plants as Black Start Resources (2019)

scores.metric_value: ~5 %/min ramp (EUR 3-5 %/min; EPR up to 5 %/min); synchronous inertia = 1; black-start = 0
scores.rationale: Slow ramp (norm 0.083) and no black-start (needs offsite/external power to restart), but it does provide true synchronous inertia, lifting the composite to ~0.29.

sources.name: World Nuclear Association - Advanced Reactors (EPR 2.5-5 %/min load-following)
POWER Magazine - Flexible Operation of Nuclear Power Plants (up to 5 %RTP/min)

scores.metric_value: ~15 %/min ramp (flash ~2-5 %/min; binary/ORC up to 15-30 %/min in flexible mode); synchronous inertia = 1; black-start = 0
scores.rationale: Moderate ramp for flexible binary/ORC (norm 0.25) plus synchronous inertia (1); not a standard black-start resource (0), giving ~0.38.

sources.name: Geothermal Rising - Firm and Flexible Power Services from Geothermal (ORC 15 %/min standard, 30 %/min flexible)
Exergy - Geothermal ORC binary cycle flexibility

scores.metric_value: Ramp effectively instantaneous (battery full power in <1 s / ~250 ms -> norm capped at 1.0); synchronous inertia = 0; black-start = 1
scores.rationale: Inverter response is essentially instantaneous (norm 1.0) and grid-forming BESS can black-start (1), but IBRs provide only synthetic, not true synchronous, inertia (0) -> 0.75.

sources.name: POWER Magazine - Benefits of Battery Storage-Based Black-Start Capability (full power <1 s)
Medium/explainer - synthetic inertia: inverter-based PV/wind/batteries provide no real (synchronous) inertia

scores.technology: Solar plus wind with long-duration storage
scores.metric_value: Ramp effectively instantaneous (inverter/electrochemical full power in <1 s -> norm capped at 1.0); synchronous inertia = 0; black-start = 1
scores.rationale: Same inverter-coupled flexibility as 4-h battery: instantaneous ramp (1.0) + grid-forming black-start (1), but only synthetic (not synchronous) inertia (0) -> 0.75; LDS adds duration, not faster ramp/inertia.

sources.name: POWER Magazine - Benefits of Battery Storage-Based Black-Start Capability
enspired - non-frequency ancillary services: inverters provide inertia only if grid-forming; not native synchronous

scores.metric_value: ~10 %/min ramp (NREL 8-10 %/min for combined cycle); synchronous inertia = 1; black-start = 0
scores.rationale: Modest ramp (norm 0.167) plus true synchronous inertia (1); standard CCGTs are not black-start capable without extra equipment (0) -> 0.33.

sources.name: NREL Greening the Grid - CCGT 8-10 %/min, OCGT up to 15 %/min
Power Engineering - Gas Turbine Combined Cycle Fast Start (no black-start; ~30 min hot start)

normalization: Linear: score = 1 − (fuel_share_percent / 100). End-points: 0% fuel share -> 1.0 (fully fuel-secure); 100% fuel share -> 0.0. The highest observed share (gas CCGT, ~65%) therefore maps to ~0.35. Scores clipped to [0,1].

scores.metric_value: 0% of LCOE (fuel cost = $0/MWh; water is free)
scores.rationale: Fuel is free-flowing water — no continuously-supplied or imported fuel; Lazard's deconstruction assigns $0 fuel cost to all renewable resources, and hydro carries no fuel line item.

sources.name: Lazard LCOE+ v18.0 (June 2025), LCOE Components by Capital/Fixed O&M/Variable O&M/Fuel, pp.39-40
EIA, Levelized Costs of New Generation Resources in the AEO2025 (Apr 2025) - hydroelectric LCOE has no fuel component

scores.metric_value: ~15% of LCOE (range: WNA 15-20% for new build; Lazard $9/MWh fuel of $141-220 total = 4-6%)
scores.rationale: Nuclear is capital-dominated; fuel (uranium, conversion, enrichment, fabrication) is a minor, low-volatility share — WNA reports 15-20% for new build, and LCOE is barely affected by a 50% fuel-price swing; SMRs inherit the same low fuel intensity.

sources.name: World Nuclear Association, Economics of Nuclear Power - fuel 15-20% of new-build LCOE, 30-40% for operating plants
Lazard LCOE+ v18.0 (June 2025), p.39-40: U.S. Nuclear fuel cost = $9/MWh of $141-220 total LCOE

scores.metric_value: 0% of LCOE (fuel cost = $0/MWh; geothermal heat is the free resource)
scores.rationale: Earth heat is the free fuel — Lazard's deconstruction shows geothermal with $0 fuel cost ($55-82 capital + $2 fixed O&M + $9-25 variable O&M, no fuel line); no imported fuel exposure.

sources.name: Lazard LCOE+ v18.0 (June 2025), p.39-40 and Key Assumptions p.35: geothermal Fuel Price = '-' (none)

scores.metric_value: 0% of LCOE (fuel cost = $0/MWh; sun and wind are free, $0/MWh charging cost)
scores.rationale: Solar PV and wind have zero fuel cost in Lazard's deconstruction, and the 4-hour battery is charged at $0/MWh from the paired renewable — added cost is capital/O&M, not fuel; no fuel-supply exposure.

sources.name: Lazard LCOE+ v18.0 (June 2025), p.39-40 (solar/wind $0 fuel) and p.36 Hybrid Generation+Storage: Charging Cost = $0.00/MWh
EIA AEO2025 LCOE report - solar PV and wind LCOE contain no fuel component

scores.technology: Solar plus wind with long-duration storage
scores.metric_value: 0% of LCOE (fuel cost = $0/MWh; renewables free, storage charged from free renewable energy)
scores.rationale: Same zero-fuel solar/wind generation as the 4-hour case; long-duration storage shifts energy in time but adds capital/O&M, not fuel — it carries the zero-fuel-cost advantage of wind and solar with no imported-fuel exposure.

sources.name: Lazard LCOE+ v18.0 (June 2025), p.39-40: solar PV and wind fuel cost = $0/MWh
NREL, Exploring the Future Energy Value of Long-Duration Energy Storage (fy25osti/92836) - LDES manages the zero-fuel-cost advantage of wind and solar

scores.metric_value: ~65% of LCOE (Lazard fuel $22/MWh of $48 at high utilization = 46% in today's low-gas environment; historically 60-80% of LCOE / ~80% of operating cost)
scores.rationale: Fuel is the single largest CCGT cost and the most volatile: Lazard's deconstruction shows fuel as ~46% of the low-end (90% capacity factor) LCOE even at a low $3.45/MMBtu gas price, with the literature commonly placing the share at 60-80% in normal gas-price environments — highest fuel-supply/import exposure of the six.

sources.name: Lazard LCOE+ v18.0 (June 2025), p.39: Gas Combined Cycle fuel cost = $22/MWh of $48 LCOE (low end); p.40 $23 of $109 (high end)
World Nuclear Association, Economics of Nuclear Power - notes gas/coal LCOE far more fuel-sensitive than nuclear (LCOE ~7% change per 10% fuel-price change)
EIA AEO2025 LCOE report - combined-cycle 'levelized variable O&M' (incl. fuel) is the dominant operating-cost segment

metric: Equal-weight composite (0-1) of three sub-metrics: (a) overnight capex intensity in USD/kW [lower is better]; (b) cost-of-capital LCOE sensitivity = % rise in LCOE when WACC goes from ~3-4% to ~10-12% [lower is better]; (c) share of the technology's global resource/fuel potential located in emerging & developing economies (EMDEs), in % [higher is better]. EMDE relevance: IEA Cost of Capital Observatory finds WACC is 2-3x higher in EMDEs than advanced economies (financing ~50% of solar LCOE in EMDEs vs 25-30% elsewhere), so capital-heavy + CoC-sensitive techs are doubly penalized where capital is dear.
normalization: Each sub-metric mapped to 0-1, then averaged (1/3 each). (a) score_capex = clamp((10000 - capex_USD/kW)/(10000 - 800), 0, 1): 800 USD/kW->1.0, 10000 USD/kW->0.0. (b) score_coc = clamp((130 - sens_pct)/(130 - 5), 0, 1): +5% LCOE rise->1.0, +130% rise->0.0. (c) score_res = EMDE_share_pct/100: 0%->0.0, 100%->1.0. Composite = (score_capex + score_coc + score_res)/3. Reported metric_value is the composite; score_0_1 equals the composite.

scores.metric_value: composite 0.83 (capex ~500 USD/kW refurb of sunk fleet -> capex 1.00; CoC sensitivity ~20% -> 0.88; EMDE resource ~60% -> 0.60)
scores.rationale: Existing fleet is already built so its capex is effectively sunk (only ~500/kW refurbishment), and most of the world's remaining hydro potential and capacity sits in EMDEs across Africa, Asia and Latin America.

sources.name: IEA - Hydroelectricity (untapped potential in developing economies)
IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024 (hydropower TIC 2,267 USD/kW, LCOE 0.057/kWh)

scores.metric_value: composite 0.14 (capex 7,861 USD/kW -> 0.23; CoC sensitivity +125% LCOE 3%->10% -> 0.04; EMDE resource/deployment ~15% -> 0.15)
scores.rationale: Highest capex (EIA $7,861/kW; FOAK ~$9,000/kW) and the single most cost-of-capital-sensitive technology (OECD-NEA: LCOE +125% from 3% to 10% discount rate), so EMDE-level WACC is crippling; supply chains and demonstrated build capability are concentrated in advanced economies.

sources.name: EIA Capital Cost Study (S&L 2023): advanced nuclear $7,861/kW, SMR $8,936/kW
World Nuclear Association - Economics of Nuclear Power (LCOE +125% from 3%->10% discount rate; capital ~60% of LCOE)
IEA Cost of Capital Observatory (WACC 2-3x higher in EMDEs)

scores.metric_value: composite 0.56 (capex 4,015 USD/kW -> 0.65; CoC sensitivity ~70% -> 0.48; EMDE resource ~55% -> 0.55)
scores.rationale: Conventional hydrothermal resource is genuinely EMDE-rich (Indonesia ~40% of global, plus Philippines, East African Rift, Latin America), but high drilling capex (IRENA $4,015/kW) makes it capital-intensive and exposed to EMDE financing costs; next-gen EGS shifts resource toward advanced economies.

sources.name: IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024 (geothermal TIC 4,015 USD/kW)
Carnegie Endowment - Unlocking Global Geothermal Energy (Indonesia ~40% of global resource; Ring of Fire / East Africa)
IRENA - East African Rift geothermal potential >20,000 MWe

scores.metric_value: composite 0.76 (blended firm capex ~2,400 USD/kW [solar 691 + wind 1,041 + 4h battery ~1,744] -> 0.83; CoC sensitivity ~35% -> 0.76; EMDE resource ~70% -> 0.70)
scores.rationale: Modest, modular capex (IRENA: solar $691/kW, wind $1,041/kW; EIA 4h battery ~$436/kWh) and the best solar/wind technical potential is in EMDE deserts and rift zones (Africa, MENA, Latin America, India), though renewables carry ~2x the CoC sensitivity of gas, which bites at EMDE WACC.

sources.name: IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024 (solar 691, onshore wind 1,041 USD/kW; battery 192 USD/kWh)
World Bank Global Photovoltaic Power Potential by Country (highest PV potential in sub-Saharan Africa & MENA)
Financing costs and the competitiveness of renewable power (renewables ~2:1 CoC sensitivity vs CCGT)

scores.metric_value: composite 0.70 (blended firm capex ~3,500 USD/kW with long-duration storage -> 0.71; CoC sensitivity ~45% -> 0.68; EMDE resource ~70% -> 0.70)
scores.rationale: Same EMDE-rich solar/wind resource base, but adding long-duration storage raises upfront capital and therefore cost-of-capital exposure relative to the 4-hour-battery variant, lowering its EMDE suitability somewhat under 2-3x WACC.

sources.name: IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024 (renewable TIC; storage capex)
EIA Capital Cost Study (4h battery $1,744/kW = $436/kWh; longer durations scale capex up)
IEA Cost of Capital Observatory dashboard (EMDE battery WACC 9-16%)

scores.metric_value: composite 0.92 (capex ~950 USD/kW -> 0.98; CoC sensitivity ~9% -> 0.97; EMDE gas reserves ~80% -> 0.80)
scores.rationale: Lowest-capex firm option (EIA combined-cycle $868-921/kW) and least cost-of-capital-sensitive (fuel-dominated LCOE rises only ~9% at high WACC), and ~80% of global gas reserves sit in EMDEs/non-OECD - the best fit for high-WACC economies (climate caveat aside).

sources.name: EIA Capital Cost Study (S&L 2023): combined-cycle $868-921/kW
Financing costs and the competitiveness of renewable power (CCGT only ~6-9% LCOE rise vs renewables 13-18%)
List of countries by natural gas proven reserves (~80% of reserves in non-OECD/EMDEs)

metric: Land-use / footprint intensity = land area occupied per unit of annual electricity generation, in m^2 per MWh/yr (equivalently 100 x ha per TWh/yr, or m^2.yr/MWh). This is an explicit land-footprint PROXY for siting freedom: lower land per MWh = easier to site = higher score. A separate geographic resource-binding note is added per technology because a low footprint does not by itself confer siting freedom if the technology can only be built where a specific resource exists (hydro head/river, geothermal heat). Values use total-area (footprint + spacing) figures where available, anchored to the peer-reviewed LUIE dataset (Van Zalk & Behrens, PLOS One 2018, median ha/TWh/y x 0.01 = m^2/MWh) and cross-checked against NREL (Ong et al. 2013) and Our World in Data.
normalization: Logarithmic (the metric spans >3 orders of magnitude, so a linear scale would collapse nuclear, gas, hydro and geothermal into ~0). score = (log10(L_max) - log10(L)) / (log10(L_max) - log10(L_min)), clipped to [0,1], where L = land-use intensity in m^2 per MWh/yr, L_min = 0.05 m^2/MWh (score = 1.0, best/lowest footprint) and L_max = 100 m^2/MWh (score = 0.0, worst/highest footprint). Denominator log10(100/0.05) = 3.301. Lower land footprint => higher score. NOTE: score reflects land footprint ONLY; geographic resource-binding is reported as a qualitative caveat in each rationale and does not alter the numeric score.

scores.metric_value: ~6.5 m^2/MWh (650 ha/TWh/y, LUIE median for single-purpose reservoir dams; mean is far higher ~15,000 ha/TWh/y for flat-terrain reservoirs)
scores.rationale: Powerhouse is tiny but reservoir flooding gives a moderate median footprint, and hydro is the MOST resource-bound of the six (needs specific river/head/topography), so practical siting freedom is low despite a middling land number.

sources.name: Van Zalk & Behrens, Land-use intensity of electricity (LUIE), PLOS One 2018 (PMC)
Our World in Data: Land use per energy source

scores.metric_value: ~0.071 m^2/MWh (7.1 ha/TWh/y, LUIE median; lowest of all electricity sources)
scores.rationale: Lowest land footprint of any source (50x less than coal, 18-27x less than ground solar) and geographically near-unconstrained (needs only cooling water/grid access), making it the most siting-flexible on a land basis; SMRs further shrink per-site land.

sources.name: Van Zalk & Behrens, LUIE, PLOS One 2018 (PMC)
Our World in Data: Land use per energy source

scores.metric_value: ~0.45 m^2/MWh (45 ha/TWh/y, LUIE median; some studies report lower, ~0.05-0.4 m^2/MWh)
scores.rationale: Very small powerhouse/wellfield footprint (second-lowest land number), but conventional geothermal is strongly resource-bound to hydrothermal zones; EGS widens the geography somewhat yet still favors high-heat-flow regions, so footprint is excellent while geographic freedom is moderate.

sources.name: Van Zalk & Behrens, LUIE, PLOS One 2018 (PMC)
Breakthrough Institute: Land-use intensity of energy sources

scores.metric_value: ~55 m^2/MWh (blended portfolio: ground solar PV ~20 m^2/MWh [2,000 ha/TWh/y] + onshore wind total/spacing ~120 m^2/MWh [12,000 ha/TWh/y]; 4h battery adds negligible land)
scores.rationale: Generation footprint is dominated by sprawling wind spacing and ground-mounted solar (highest land per MWh of the six); battery itself is land-dense, so the portfolio is land-hungry though geographically widely available.

sources.name: Van Zalk & Behrens, LUIE, PLOS One 2018 (PMC)
NREL Ong et al. 2013, Land-Use Requirements for Solar Power Plants (OSTI)

scores.technology: Solar plus wind with long-duration storage
scores.metric_value: ~65 m^2/MWh (same solar/wind generation footprint as above but with higher renewable over-build to firm multi-day demand; LDES media e.g. pumped hydro/CAES add modest land per delivered MWh)
scores.rationale: Highest land footprint of the six: long-duration firming requires extra renewable over-build (more wind/solar acreage) plus the LDES facility itself; geographically broad but the most land-intensive option.

sources.name: Van Zalk & Behrens, LUIE, PLOS One 2018 (PMC)
NREL Ong et al. 2013, Land-Use Requirements for Solar Power Plants (OSTI)

scores.metric_value: ~6 m^2/MWh (LUIE median natural gas: 410 ha/TWh/y footprint [4.1 m^2/MWh] to 1,900 ha/TWh/y total incl. upstream/spacing [19 m^2/MWh]; representative total ~6 m^2/MWh)
scores.rationale: Plant footprint itself is very small; the bulk of land is diffuse upstream gas extraction, and CCGTs can be sited almost anywhere with pipeline/grid access, so geographic freedom is high while the total-area land number is moderate.

sources.name: Van Zalk & Behrens, LUIE, PLOS One 2018 (PMC)
Breakthrough Institute: Land-use intensity of energy sources

metric: Maximum continuous firm-output autonomy without any external energy input, in hours (assessed on a log10 scale)
normalization: Score = clamp( log10(hours) / log10(100000), 0, 1 ) = log10(hours) / 5. Fixed numeric end-points: 1 hour of autonomy -> 0.0; 100,000 hours (~11.4 years, the practical ceiling representing an effectively unlimited on-site resource) -> 1.0. Reference points on this scale: 4 h -> 0.12, 24 h -> 0.28, 50 h -> 0.34, 1,000 h -> 0.60, 13,000 h -> 0.82, 100,000 h -> 1.00.

scores.metric_value: ~1,000+ h (weeks to months for the large-reservoir share; seasonal storage operating independent of inflow)
scores.rationale: Large storage reservoirs can run firm independent of hydrological inflow for many weeks or months; using ~1,000 h (the large-reservoir multi-week/seasonal share) gives log10(1000)/5 = 0.60.

sources.name: IHA - Types of Hydropower (storage hydropower: independent of inflow for weeks or months)

scores.metric_value: ~13,000 h (18-24 month fuel core; continuous run between refueling outages)
scores.rationale: Reactors operate 18-24 months between refuelings; an 18-month core is ~13,140 h of fully self-contained firm output, giving log10(13140)/5 = 0.82 (near the top of the scale).

sources.name: World Nuclear Association - Nuclear Power Reactors (refuelling at 12, 18 or 24 month intervals)

scores.metric_value: effectively unlimited (resource is the fuel); capped at 100,000 h for scaling; ~90% capacity factor, baseload 24/7
scores.rationale: Geothermal's energy source is the in-situ heat resource, so there is no on-site fuel inventory to deplete; DOE calls it always-on baseload (~90% CF), so it pins the upper end of the scale (capped at 100,000 h -> 1.00).

sources.name: DOE Geothermal Technologies Office (baseload, ~90% capacity factor, around-the-clock)

scores.metric_value: ~4 h (lithium-ion utility-scale standard discharge duration)
scores.rationale: The 4-hour Li-ion battery is the autonomy ceiling once VRE input stops; EIA notes utility batteries average ~3-4 h, so 4 h -> log10(4)/5 = 0.12.

sources.name: EIA Today in Energy - Duration of utility-scale batteries (~3.0 h avg, 4-8 h diurnal)

scores.technology: Solar plus wind with long-duration storage
scores.metric_value: ~50 h (LDES band 10 to ~100 h; DOE >=10 h, ARPA-E DAYS 10-100 h)
scores.rationale: DOE defines LDES as >=10 h and ARPA-E's DAYS program targets 10 to ~100 h at rated power; using ~50 h (mid-band) gives log10(50)/5 = 0.34.

sources.name: DOE OCED - Long-Duration Energy Storage (>=10 hours)
ARPA-E DAYS Program Overview (10 to ~100 hours at rated power)

scores.metric_value: ~24 h (pipeline-dependent; hours with no on-site storage, up to ~2-3 days on-site oil for dual-fuel units)
scores.rationale: A pure pipeline-fed CCGT has essentially no on-site fuel (hours); dual-fuel units credited under ISO-NE's program hold ~2-3 days of on-site oil, so ~24 h is representative -> log10(24)/5 = 0.28.

sources.name: ISO New England - Inventoried Energy Program (on-site fuel ~2-3 days for cold-period reliability)
EIA Today in Energy - EIA now publishes oil stocks at power plant level (plants keep oil stocks on hand for peak dispatch)
